revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:舟形木棺]]
'''Boat-Shaped Wooden Coffin''' (舟形木棺) refers to a type of {{ill|wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}} found in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} and {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. These coffins were carved from logs split lengthwise into two parts. The interior was hollowed out to form the coffin body and lid, which were combined to create an elongated cylindrical coffin shaped like a boat.
== Overview == Logs were split in half, hollowed on the inside, and the exterior was carved and smoothed to create the coffin body and lid. This type of coffin is referred to as a carved wooden coffin. Among these, coffins with an exterior bottom shaped like a "boat bottom" are specifically called boat-shaped wooden coffins. In the Kofun period, these coffins are further classified into two types: - '''Type 1 Boat-Shaped Wooden Coffin:''' The cross-section of the coffin body forms a gentle U-shape without distinguishing the bow (prow) and stern (aft). - '''Type 2 Boat-Shaped Wooden Coffin:''' The coffin body has a U-shaped cross-section, with a clear distinction between the bow and stern.
Type 1 boat-shaped wooden coffins were adopted in the early Kofun period, primarily in prominent Kofuns around the Kinai region. Few examples of split bamboo-shaped wooden coffins were used in significant Kofuns of the same era, suggesting that the boat-shaped wooden coffin was the highest-Ranking coffin type during the early Kofun period. Type 2 boat-shaped wooden coffins were used throughout the Kofun period, distributed mainly in eastern Japan and nationwide. These are thought to be modeled after {{ill|Dugout canoe|en|Dugout canoe|qid=Q596073|lt=dugout canoes|1=Dugout canoe}} or {{ill|semi-structured boats|ja|準構造船|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/semi-structured boats|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the Yayoi period, coffins with "boat bottom-shaped" exterior bottoms are also called boat-shaped wooden coffins. However, many examples from the later Yayoi period in the {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|qid=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}} and {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|qid=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}} regions display different characteristics compared to the Type 1 coffins from Kinai. This distinction has led to the proposal of the concept of "boat-bottom-shaped wooden coffins."
== Kofuns with Boat-Shaped Wooden Coffins == * {{ill|Hirate Town Ruins|ja|平手町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirate Town Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平手町|1=Hirate-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17226501}} (Yayoi period, {{ill|Square Moat Burial|ja|方形周溝墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Moat Burial|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487466}}, {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|qid=Q11751|lt=Nagoya, Aichi|1=Nagoya}}) * {{ill|Yukinoyama Kofun|en|Yukinoyama Kofun|qid=Q11659952|lt=Yukinoyama Kofun|1=Yukinoyama Kofun}} (Early Kofun period, {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|qid=Q240891|lt=Higashiomi, Shiga|1=Higashiōmi}}. Found with three {{ill|triangular-rimmed deity-and-beast mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed deity-and-beast mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, coffin length of 5.2 meters, width 0.9 meters at the north end, 0.8 meters at the south end, semi-circular rope-patterned protrusions on both ends, and the coffin interior divided into three sections by partition boards.) * {{ill|Shimozuke Nanamawari Kagamitsuka Kofun|ja|下野七廻り鏡塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimozuke Nanamawari Kagamitsuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Kofun period, {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, {{ill|Tochigi (city)|en|Tochigi (city)|qid=Q44830|lt=Tochigi City|1=Tochigi (city)}}. The boat-shaped wooden coffin is designated an Important Cultural Property and is displayed at the {{ill|Tochigi City Ohira History and Folklore Museum|ja|栃木市おおひら歴史民俗資料館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi City Ohira History and Folklore Museum|qq=}}.<ref>{{cite book|和書|editor=栃木県歴史散歩編集委員会|title=栃木県の歴史散歩|series=歴史散歩⑨|publisher={{ill|Yamakawa Publishing|ja|山川出版社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamakawa Publishing|1=Yamakawa Shuppansha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11468274}}|page=226|isbn=978-4-634-24609-6}}</ref>)
== References == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Coffin|en|Coffin|qid=Q184418|lt=Coffin|1=Coffin}} * {{ill|Pit-Type Burial Chambers|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit-Type Burial Chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun Period|1=Kofun period}} * {{ill|Split Bamboo-Shaped Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Split Bamboo-Shaped Wooden Coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} * {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Hajime Ōtsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Ōtsuka|1=Hatsushige Ōtsuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11433969}} and {{ill|Saburō Kobayashi (Archaeologist)|ja|小林三郎 (考古学者)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Kobayashi (Archaeologist)|1=Saburō Kobayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11461352}}. *Kofun Dictionary* (古墳辞典), Tokyo: {{ill|Tokyo-do Publishing|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo-do Publishing|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11524645}}, December 1982. ISBN 4-490-10165-1. * Kosaku Obayashi. *Structure and Genealogy of Coffins in the Kofun Period* (古墳時代棺槨の構造と系譜), December 2018.
== External Links == * [http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/SearchDetail.do?heritageId=126308 Cultural Property Online: Shimozuke Nanamawari Kagamitsuka Kofun]

{{Kofun navbar}}
[[File:Hirate-cho ruins-Hokei shukobo02.jpg|230px|thumb|{{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|qid=Q959782|lt=Excavation|1=Archaeological excavation}} of a {{ill|Square Moat Burial|ja|方形周溝墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Moat Burial|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487466}} at the {{ill|Hirate Town Ruins|ja|平手町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirate Town Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平手町|1=Hirate-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17226501}}. The traces of a boat-shaped wooden coffin are visible.]]
{{translated page|ja|舟形木棺|version=100444318|comment=Imported full ja history}}
